message.content:
Adversarially verify this finding about a design doc (the doc is at /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt; the repo is /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner; you may use WebSearch/WebFetch and read files). Try to REFUTE it. Finding from the "facts" reviewer, severity major:
TITLE: The design largely reinvents existing wheels — gha-outrunner itself plus GARM, GitHub's official scaleset client, RunsOn, actuated, Ubicloud, WarpBuild
DETAIL: VERIFIED: The proposed gha-isolated adds only platform detection, backend selection, config generation, and service install on top of gha-outrunner — which already does the hard part (ephemeral runners, scaleset registration, Docker/libvirt/Tart isolation, no k8s: https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner). Established alternatives covering the same space: (1) GARM — Cloudbase's multi-cloud auto-scaling manager for GitHub Actions/Gitea self-hosted runners with pluggable providers, mature and widely used (https://github.com/cloudbase/garm). (2) GitHub's official standalone Runner Scale Set Client, actions/scaleset, public preview Feb 5 2026 — a Go module for building custom autoscalers on VMs/containers/bare metal WITHOUT Kubernetes (https://github.com/actions/scaleset, https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/186265). (3) Commercial/hosted: RunsOn (runs in your AWS account), actuated (microVMs on your hardware), Ubicloud, WarpBuild, Namespace, Blacksmith (https://runs-on.com/alternatives-to/github-actions-runners/, https://betterstack.com/community/comparisons/actuated-alternatives/). (4) ARC remains the k8s reference implementation (https://github.com/actions/actions-runner-controller). Cirrus Labs' own runner products are shutting down after the OpenAI acquisition (Cirrus CI ends June 1, 2026: https://www.warpbuild.com/blog/cirrus-ci-shutting-down). The unique value of gha-isolated is only the thin cross-platform 'pick the best backend automatically + install as a service' UX.
RECOMMENDATION: Before building, evaluate GARM (with libvirt/LXD providers) and gha-outrunner-as-is with a small install script. If proceeding, scope gha-isolated as a thin installer/supervisor and consider upstreaming auto-detection and service install to gha-outrunner (MIT, would likely welcome contributions) instead of a separate binary.
Is the finding factually correct and does the recommendation make sense? If the factual basis is wrong or the severity is inflated, refute it. Default to refuted=true only if you have concrete contrary evidence or the claim is unsupported after checking.

input.prompt: Does this repo exist? What does it do? Does it support ephemeral runners, GitHub scale set registration, and Docker/libvirt/Tart isolation backends without Kubernetes? What license?
Does this repo exist? What does GARM do? Does it auto-scale self-hosted GitHub Actions runners with pluggable providers (LXD, libvirt, clouds)? Is it mature/active?
Does this repo exist? Is it GitHub's official standalone Runner Scale Set Client Go module for building custom autoscalers without Kubernetes? When was it released / is it public preview?

content.content:
# gha-outrunner Repository Analysis

**Does the repo exist?** Yes, it's a public repository at `NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner` with 84 commits, 5 releases, and 3 stars.

**What does it do?** The project provides "Ephemeral GitHub Actions runners, no Kubernetes required." It provisions fresh Docker containers or VMs for each job, then destroys them afterward using GitHub's scaleset API.

**Ephemeral runners & scale set registration?** Yes. The tool "uses GitHub's scaleset API to register as an autoscaling runner group" and creates ephemeral execution environments that are cleaned up post-job.

**Isolation backends?** Yes, it supports three provisioners:
- **Docker** (Linux/macOS hosts, Linux runners)
- **libvirt** (Linux hosts, Windows/Linux runners via KVM VMs)
- **Tart** (macOS Apple Silicon hosts, macOS/ARM64 Linux runners)

**Kubernetes requirement?** No. The developers explicitly state: "If you're running on bare metal or a simple VPS, you shouldn't need a cluster just to get ephemeral runners."

**License?** MIT license

# GitHub Actions Runner Scale Set Client

Yes, this repository exists. It's Anthropic's official standalone Go client for GitHub Actions Runner Scale Set APIs.

**Status:** The project is in "Public Preview" — the API is stable, but "interfaces and examples in this repository may change."

**Purpose:** This package enables platform teams and infrastructure providers to "build their own custom autoscaling solutions for GitHub Actions runners" without requiring Kubernetes or the full controller.

**Release:** The repository shows 4 releases, with v0.4.0 listed as the latest (dated May 5, 2026). It requires Go 1.25 or later.

**Key capability:** Developers can create scale sets, generate just-in-time runner configs, and manage message sessions using primitives extracted from the actions-runner-controller project.

# GARM Repository Analysis

**Does this repo exist?** Yes, it's the official cloudbase/garm repository on GitHub with 346 stars and 49 forks.

**What does GARM do?** GARM is a self-hosted runner manager that "automatically creates, scales, and destroys ephemeral runner instances across multiple clouds and infrastructure providers from a single controller." It supports GitHub Actions, GitHub Enterprise Server, and Gitea Actions.

**Auto-scaling with pluggable providers?** Yes, exactly. The project supports numerous providers including AWS EC2, Azure, GCP, OpenStack, OCI, LXD, Incus, Kubernetes, and others. "Providers are standalone executables" that can even be written in custom languages, and the architecture includes "scale to zero" capability for on-demand pools.

**Maturity/Activity?** The project appears active and reasonably mature. It has 13 releases (latest v0.2.1 from May 2026), comprehensive documentation, a Kubernetes operator integration, 1,493 commits on main, and an active Slack community. However, the repository notes that the main branch "is not guaranteed to be stable"—users should use tagged releases for production deployments.

input.query: Cirrus Labs OpenAI acquisition Cirrus CI shutting down June 2026 Tart

input.prompt: What is this discussion about? Is it the announcement of the standalone Runner Scale Set Client (actions/scaleset) public preview? What date was it announced?

content.content:
# GitHub Actions Runner Scale Set Client Announcement

Yes, this discussion is the announcement of the **GitHub Actions Runner Scale Set Client** public preview release.

**Announcement Date:** February 3, 2026

**Overview:** The discussion introduces a new "standalone Go-based module that lets you build custom autoscaling solutions for GitHub Actions runners without requiring Kubernetes." This tool enables developers to implement autoscaling across diverse infrastructure platforms—containers, virtual machines, and bare metal—while maintaining full control over runner provisioning.

**Key distinction:** Unlike the Actions Runner Controller (which remains the recommended Kubernetes solution), this new client provides "a new tool for interfacing with same scale set APIs for building custom autoscaling solutions outside of Kubernetes."

The announcement emphasizes platform flexibility, multi-label support, and agentic workflow compatibility, while clarifying that users bear responsibility for their own infrastructure automation and provisioning logic.

Web search results for query: "Cirrus Labs OpenAI acquisition Cirrus CI shutting down June 2026 Tart"

Links: [{"title":"Cirrus Labs to join OpenAI","url":"https://cirruslabs.org/"},{"title":"OpenAI Buys Cirrus Labs Because AI Agents Need Sandboxes — Agent Wars","url":"https://agent-wars.com/news/2026-04-11-cirrus-labs-to-join-openai-cirrus-ci-shutting-down-june-1-2026"},{"title":"Cirrus CI Is Shutting Down: Migrate to GitHub Actions + WarpBuild - WarpBuild Blog","url":"https://www.warpbuild.com/blog/cirrus-ci-shutting-down"},{"title":"Cirrus is shutting down: moving to Bitrise Build Hub is your best option - Bitrise Blog","url":"https://bitrise.io/blog/post/cirrus-ci-shutting-down-move-to-build-hub"},{"title":"Cirrus CI is shutting down: upgrade to a scalable, AI-ready alternative - CircleCI","url":"https://circleci.com/blog/cirrus-ci-alternative/"},{"title":"Cirrus Labs Joins OpenAI: CI/CD Shutdown June 1 | byteiota","url":"https://byteiota.com/cirrus-labs-joins-openai-ci-cd-shutdown-june-1/"},{"title":"OpenAI Acquires Cirrus Labs for Agent Infrastructure, Shuts Down Cirrus CI | SimpleNews.ai","url":"https://www.simplenews.ai/news/openai-acquires-cirrus-labs-for-agent-infrastructure-shuts-down-cirrus-ci-3fyd"},{"title":"Cirrus Labs is joining OpenAI: What this means for the future of Tart and Orchard","url":"https://macstadium.com/blog/cirrus-labs-is-joining-openai"},{"title":"Cirrus Labs Acquired by OpenAI, CI Business to Close in June 2026 | Trending Stories | HyperAI","url":"https://beta.hyper.ai/en/stories/b3d3a83e59314f2d514382873e0d0610"}]

Based on the search results, here's what happened with Cirrus Labs, OpenAI, and the shutdown:

## Key Points

**The Acquisition**: OpenAI has acquired Cirrus Labs, makers of Cirrus CI and the Tart virtualization tool, for their Agent Infrastructure team.

**Shutdown Timeline**: Cirrus CI will shut down effective Monday, June 1, 2026. This was announced on April 7, 2026.

**Why OpenAI Acquired Cirrus Labs**: The deal gives OpenAI sandboxing tech for safely running AI-generated code in isolated environments. Joining OpenAI allows us to extend the mission we started with Cirrus Labs: building new kinds of tooling and environments that make engineers more effective, for both human engineers and agentic engineers.

**What Happens to Tools**: In the coming weeks, we will relicense all of our source-available tools, including Tart, Vetu and Orchard under a more permissive license. We have also stopped charging licensing fees for them.

**About Tart**: In 2022, we built Tart, which became the most popular virtualization solution for Apple Silicon, along with several other tools along the way.

**Customer Impact**: We are no longer accepting new customers for Cirrus Runners but will continue supporting the service for existing customers through their existing contract periods.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.reasoning: Adversarial verification FAILED to refute the finding — every material factual claim checks out. (1) gha-outrunner (NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner, MIT) verified: ephemeral runners via GitHub's scaleset API with Docker/libvirt/Tart backends, no Kubernetes — it already does everything hard in the design. The design doc itself describes gha-isolated as 'a wrapper CLI around gha-outrunner,' and its skeleton confirms the thin-layer characterization: runner.rs is a placeholder that shells out to gha-outrunner; the only real logic is platform detection, backend selection, YAML config generation, and (unimplemented) service install. (2) GARM (cloudbase/garm) verified: active (v0.2.1 May 2026), auto-scaling ephemeral runners with pluggable providers (LXD, Incus, AWS, Azure, GCP, k8s). (3) actions/scaleset verified: GitHub's official standalone Go Runner Scale Set Client, public preview, explicitly for custom autoscalers on VMs/containers/bare metal without Kubernetes — only error is the date: announced Feb 3, 2026 (community discussion #186265), not Feb 5. (4) Cirrus Labs → OpenAI acquisition verified (announced April 7, 2026); Cirrus CI shuts down June 1, 2026, Cirrus Runners closed to new customers — the finding's 'runner products shutting down' is accurate (nuance: Tart the tool is being relicensed more permissively, not killed, but the finding didn't claim otherwise). (5) Commercial alternatives (RunsOn, actuated, Ubicloud, WarpBuild, Namespace, Blacksmith) and ARC-as-k8s-reference are all real and correctly characterized. The recommendation (evaluate GARM/gha-outrunner-as-is; scope gha-isolated as a thin installer or upstream auto-detection to MIT-licensed gha-outrunner) is sound and proportionate. Severity 'major' is justified for a build-vs-reuse design question. Only correction needed: the scaleset public-preview announcement date.
input.corrected_claim: The finding stands as written with one trivial date correction: GitHub's standalone Runner Scale Set Client (actions/scaleset) public preview was announced February 3, 2026 (community discussion #186265), not February 5, 2026. All other facts verified: gha-outrunner already provides ephemeral scaleset runners with Docker/libvirt/Tart isolation and no k8s (MIT); GARM is a mature pluggable-provider autoscaling manager; RunsOn/actuated/Ubicloud/WarpBuild cover the commercial space; ARC remains the k8s reference; Cirrus Labs' runner products are winding down after the OpenAI acquisition (Cirrus CI ends June 1, 2026; Tart itself continues under a more permissive license). gha-isolated's unique value is indeed only the thin cross-platform backend auto-detection + service-install UX on top of gha-outrunner.
